From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Honorific style name prefix

"Honourable" and "Hon." redirect here. For other uses, see [Honour](/wiki/Honour "Honour") and [Hon (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hon_(disambiguation) "Hon (disambiguation)").

For the Ugandan satire television series, see [The Honourables](/wiki/The_Honourables "The Honourables").

Not to be confused with [The Right Honourable](/wiki/The_Right_Honourable "The Right Honourable") or [The Most Honourable](/wiki/The_Most_Honourable "The Most Honourable").

***The Honourable*** ([Commonwealth English](/wiki/Commonwealth_English "Commonwealth English")) or ***The Honorable*** ([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English"); [see spelling differences](/wiki/American_and_British_English_spelling_differences#-our,_-or "American and British English spelling differences")) (abbreviation: ***Hon.***, ***Hon'ble***, or variations) is an [honorific](/wiki/Honorific "Honorific") [style](/wiki/Style_(manner_of_address) "Style (manner of address)") that is used as a prefix before the names or titles of certain people, usually with official governmental or diplomatic positions.

## Use by governments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Use by governments")]

### International diplomacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: International diplomacy")]

In international diplomatic relations, representatives of foreign states are often styled as *The Honourable*. Deputy chiefs of mission, *[chargés d'affaires](/wiki/Charg%C3%A9s_d%27affaires "Chargés d'affaires")*, consuls-general, consuls and honorary consuls are always given the style. All heads of consular posts, whether they are honorary or career postholders, are accorded the style according to the [State Department of the United States](/wiki/State_Department_of_the_United_States "State Department of the United States").[[1]](#cite_note-1) However, the style *[Excellency](/wiki/Excellency "Excellency")* instead of *The Honourable* is used for ambassadors and [high commissioners](/wiki/High_commissioner "High commissioner") only.

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Africa")]

#### Democratic Republic of the Congo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Democratic Republic of the Congo")]

In the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo"), the prefix 'Honourable' or 'Hon.' is used for members of both chambers of the [Parliament of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Parliament of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"). Informally, senators are sometimes given the higher style of 'Venerable'.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Ghana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ghana")]

The style of Honourable is accorded [members of parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ghana "Parliament of Ghana") in [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"). It is also extended to certain grades of Royal Orders awarded by Ghana's sub-national Kingdoms.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Kenya

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Kenya")]

The style Honourable is used to address members of the [Kenyan parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Kenya "Parliament of Kenya"). Traditionally, members of Parliament are not allowed to call each other by name in the chambers, but rather use the terms "Honourable colleague" or "Honourable Member for ...".[[2]](#cite_note-2) The written form is *Hon. [Last Name], [First Name]* or *Honourable [Last Name]* or *Honourable* [Position] (e.g. *Honourable Speaker*).

#### Mauritius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mauritius")]

Recipients of the rank of Grand Officer or above of the [Order of the Star and Key of the Indian Ocean](/wiki/Order_of_the_Star_and_Key_of_the_Indian_Ocean "Order of the Star and Key of the Indian Ocean") and persons knighted by Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") are automatically entitled to prefix *The Hon*, *Hons* or *The Honourable* to their name. Commanders and Officers may request permission from the President to use this prefix. Recipients of the order who are not Mauritian citizens may not use the prefix or post-nominals unless granted permission by the President. All 70 members of the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Mauritius) "National Assembly (Mauritius)") also use this prefix, including all Cabinet ministers.

#### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: South Africa")]

All members of the [South African parliament](/wiki/South_African_parliament "South African parliament") and the nine [provincial legislatures](/wiki/Provincial_legislature_(South_Africa) "Provincial legislature (South Africa)") are entitled to this prefix.

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Europe")]

#### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Germany")]

A rough equivalent of the style *Honourable* would be *[Hochwohlgeboren](/wiki/Hochwohlgeboren "Hochwohlgeboren")* 'high well-born', which was used until 1918 for all members of noble families not having any higher style. Its application to bourgeois dignitaries became common in the 19th century, though it has faded since and was always of doubtful correctness.

*Ehrwürdig* or *Ehrwürden*, the literal translation of 'honourable', is used for [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") and [religious](/wiki/Religious_(Catholicism) "Religious (Catholicism)")—with the exceptions of priests and abbesses, who are *Hochwürden* '[reverend](/wiki/Reverend "Reverend")'. A subdeacon is *Wohlehrwürden* 'very honourable'; a deacon is *Hochehrwürden* '[right honourable](/wiki/Right_honourable "Right honourable")'.

#### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ireland")]

In Ireland, all judges of the High Court, Court of Appeal and Supreme Court are referred to as *The Honourable Mr/Ms Justice*.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Italy")]

In Italy, the style *The Honourable* (Italian: *Onorevole*) is customarily used to refer to a member of the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "Chamber of Deputies (Italy)"). Former members of parliament can maintain the style.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

#### Malta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Malta")]

All members of the unicameral [Parliament of Malta](/wiki/Parliament_of_Malta "Parliament of Malta") are entitled to this prefix.

#### The Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The Netherlands")]

An extensive system of honorifics used to be in place in the Netherlands. In a more formal setting it still is. *De weledele heer/vrouwe* 'the honourable lord/lady' is used for the genteel bourgeoisie. The middle classes are instead addressed with *De heer/mevrouw* 'sir/madam', which is the equivalent of *Mr/Ms* in English.

Also typical is the use of *De weledelgeboren heer/vrouwe* 'the well-born lord/lady', for students at universities, traditionally children of the genteel bourgeoisie.

The system adds honorifics based on prestige for military officers based on rank, barristers, prosecutors, judges, members of parliament, government ministers, nobles, clergy, and for academic degrees of master's and above.

In the Dutch language, *Mr* is a formal and academic title, for both men and women, protected by Dutch law. It stands for *Meester* 'master', and is strictly reserved for holders of a master's degree in law (LL.M.) who are qualified to practice law. Holders are addressed as *De weledelgestrenge heer/vrouwe Mr* 'the honourable strict lord/lady master', followed by their name.

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Spain")]

In the Spanish Autonomous Community of [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), the style Honorable ([Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"): *Honorable*) is used for current and former members of the cabinet (*consellers*) of the Catalan Government (*[Generalitat de Catalunya](/wiki/Generalitat_de_Catalunya "Generalitat de Catalunya")*). Former and current heads of government or president of the Generalitat are given the style of *Molt Honorable* ("Very Honorable"). This also applies to current and former heads of government of the Autonomous Communities of [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia "Valencia") and [Balearic Islands](/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

##### Entitlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Entitlement")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/The_Honourable "Special:EditPage/The Honourable") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(March 2012)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In the United Kingdom, all sons and daughters of [viscounts](/wiki/Viscount "Viscount") and [barons](/wiki/Baron "Baron")[[6]](#cite_note-6) (including the holders of [life peerages](/wiki/Life_peer "Life peer")) and the younger sons of [earls](/wiki/Earl "Earl") are styled with this prefix (the daughters and younger sons of [dukes](/wiki/Duke "Duke") and [marquesses](/wiki/Marquess "Marquess") and the daughters of [earls](/wiki/Earl "Earl") have the higher style of *Lord* or *Lady* before their first names, and the eldest sons of dukes, marquesses and earls are known by one of their father's or mother's subsidiary titles). The style is only a [courtesy](/wiki/Courtesy_title "Courtesy title"), however, and on legal documents they may be described as, for instance, *John Smith, Esq., commonly called The Honourable John Smith*. As the wives of sons of peers share the styles of their husbands, the wives of the sons of viscounts and barons and the younger sons of earls are styled, for example, *The Hon. Mrs John Smith*. Likewise, the married daughters of viscounts and barons, whose husbands hold no higher title or dignity, are styled, for example, *The Hon. Mrs Smith*.

In 1912, King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") granted [maids of honour](/wiki/Maid_of_honour "Maid of honour") (royal attendants) the style of *the honourable* for life, with precedence next after daughters of barons.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

*The Honourable* is also customarily used as a form of address for most foreign nobility that is not formally recognised by the sovereign (e.g. ambassadors) when in the UK.

Some people are entitled to the prefix by virtue of their offices. Rules exist that allow certain individuals to keep the prefix *The Honourable* even after retirement.

* Judges of the [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_England_and_Wales "High Court of England and Wales") and other superior courts in the Commonwealth (if the judge is a [knight](/wiki/Knight "Knight"), the style *Sir John Smith* is used socially instead of *The Honourable Mr Justice Smith*); and
* Members of Commonwealth [executive](/wiki/Executive_Council_(Commonwealth_countries) "Executive Council (Commonwealth countries)") and [legislative councils](/wiki/Legislative_Council "Legislative Council") (or [senates](/wiki/Senate "Senate")) where the legislature is [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Several corporate entities have been awarded the style by [royal warrant](/wiki/Royal_warrant_(document) "Royal warrant (document)"), for example:

* The [Honourable Artillery Company](/wiki/Honourable_Artillery_Company "Honourable Artillery Company")
* [The Honourable East India Company](/wiki/Honourable_East_India_Company "Honourable East India Company")
* The four [Inns of Court](/wiki/Inns_of_Court "Inns of Court") (for example [The Honourable Society of the Middle Temple](/wiki/Middle_Temple "Middle Temple"))
* The [Honourable Company of Master Mariners](/wiki/Honourable_Company_of_Master_Mariners "Honourable Company of Master Mariners")
* The [Honourable Company of Air Pilots](/wiki/Honourable_Company_of_Air_Pilots "Honourable Company of Air Pilots")

##### Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Usage")]

The style *The Honourable* is usually used in addressing envelopes (where it is usually abbreviated to *The Hon.*) and formally elsewhere, in which case *Mr* or *[Esquire](/wiki/Esquire "Esquire")* are omitted. In speech, however, The Honourable John Smith is usually referred to simply as Mr John Smith.

In the [House of Commons of the United Kingdom](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), as in other traditionally lower houses of Parliament and other legislatures, members must as a minimum refer to each other as *the honourable member* or *my honourable friend* out of courtesy, but they are not entitled to the style in writing. Members who are 'senior' [barristers](/wiki/Barrister "Barrister") may be called *the honourable and learnèd member*, serving or ex-serving members of the military *the honourable and gallant member*, and ordained clergy in the House *the honourable and reverend member*, a practice that the Modernisation Committee recommended should be abolished,[[8]](#cite_note-8) but the use of which has continued.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) When anyone is entitled to be styled *[Right Honourable](/wiki/Right_Honourable_(House_of_Commons) "Right Honourable (House of Commons)")* this is used instead of *honourable*.

In the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands"), the style *the honourable* is given to any serving or former [members](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly "Member of the Legislative Assembly") of the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_the_Falkland_Islands "Legislative Assembly of the Falkland Islands") or [Legislative Council](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_the_Falkland_Islands "Legislative Council of the Falkland Islands").

In the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"), the style *the honourable* (often abbreviated to *Hon.*) is used to refer to a [minister](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_of_the_Isle_of_Man "Council of Ministers of the Isle of Man") while holding office.

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: North America")]

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Canada")]

Further information: [Style (manner of address)](/wiki/Style_(manner_of_address) "Style (manner of address)")

In Canada, while not always enshrined in legislation, some people are commonly referred to as *The Honourable* (French: *l'honorable*). Those who have the honorific for life include:[[11]](#cite_note-HEritage-styles-11)[[12]](#cite_note-PWGSC-styles-12)[[13]](#cite_note-GC-CA-Table-Of-Titles-13)

* [Senators](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada")
* Members of the [King's Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "King's Privy Council for Canada") (mostly members or former members of the federal [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada"))
* [Lieutenant governors](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant governor (Canada)")
* Members of the executive councils (provincial ministers), and living former ministers as of a specified date, of four provinces:
  + [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Nova_Scotia "Executive Council of Nova Scotia") (since 2009, initially only those who ended their ministerial service after 2009. In 2010 the privilege was extended to all living former ministers who ended their service earlier. In 2014 the privilege was further granted to six living former speakers.)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
  + [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Saskatchewan "Executive Council of Saskatchewan") (since 2019)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
  + [Alberta](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Alberta "Executive Council of Alberta") (since 2022)[[16]](#cite_note-16)
  + [Ontario](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Ontario "Executive Council of Ontario") (since 2025)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

People who have the honorific only while in office include:[[11]](#cite_note-HEritage-styles-11)[[13]](#cite_note-GC-CA-Table-Of-Titles-13)

* The [speaker of the House of Commons of Canada](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_of_Canada "Speaker of the House of Commons of Canada"), though it is customary for former speakers to be appointed to the privy council after their term
* Members of provincial and territorial [executive councils](/wiki/Executive_Council_(Commonwealth_countries) "Executive Council (Commonwealth countries)") (premiers, cabinet ministers and deputy premiers). Some executive councillors (see above) have the honorific for life.
* Speakers of provincial and territorial legislatures
* Government [house leaders](/wiki/House_leader "House leader") of provinces and territories
* Territorial commissioners
* Justices of the [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada")
* Justices of the [Federal Court](/wiki/Federal_Court_of_Canada "Federal Court of Canada") and [Federal Court of Appeals](/wiki/Federal_Court_of_Appeal_(Canada) "Federal Court of Appeal (Canada)"), [Tax Court of Canada](/wiki/Tax_Court_of_Canada "Tax Court of Canada")
* Justices of the [Court Martial of Canada](/wiki/Courts-martial_of_Canada "Courts-martial of Canada") and [Court Martial Appeal Court of Canada](/wiki/Court_Martial_Appeal_Court_of_Canada "Court Martial Appeal Court of Canada")
* Justices of provincial superior courts (trial and appellate)
* Provincial inferior court judges

Derivatives include:

* The Honourable Mr/Madam Justice — justices of federal courts, provincial appellate and superior courts.
* The Honourable Judge — judges of provincial courts and formerly judges of district or county courts.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In all cases, the [governor general of Canada](/wiki/Governor_general_of_Canada "Governor general of Canada") may grant permission to retain the style after they cease to hold office. Persons eligible to retain the style include the speaker of the House of Commons (who may already be eligible as a privy councillor), territorial commissioners, and judges of certain courts (e.g., the Supreme Court of Canada). The most recent former justice granted such privileges was [Frank Iacobucci](/wiki/Frank_Iacobucci "Frank Iacobucci").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

It is usual for speakers of the House of Commons to be made privy councillors, in which case they keep the style for life. A [leader of the Official Opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Official_Opposition_(Canada) "Leader of the Official Opposition (Canada)") who has not previously been a cabinet minister may be appointed to the Privy Council, particularly at times of military conflict or other national security crises, so that they may be given security clearance, granting them the style (being the only non-government MP accorded such privilege). In the past, certain provincial premiers (e.g., [Peter Lougheed](/wiki/Peter_Lougheed "Peter Lougheed"), [Bill Davis](/wiki/Bill_Davis "Bill Davis"), [Joey Smallwood](/wiki/Joey_Smallwood "Joey Smallwood") and [Tommy Douglas](/wiki/Tommy_Douglas "Tommy Douglas")) were elevated to the Privy Council and gained the style, but such practice is rare.

Members of the [House of Commons of Canada](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") and of provincial legislatures refer to each other during proceedings of the house with the courtesy style "honourable member" (or *l'honorable député*), but their name is not otherwise prefixed with *the Honourable* (unless they are privy councillors or executive councillors).[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Current and former [governors general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada"), [prime ministers](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada"), [chief justices of Canada](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_Canada "Chief Justice of Canada") and certain other eminent persons use the style of *[Right Honourable](/wiki/The_Right_Honourable_(Canada) "The Right Honourable (Canada)")* for life (or *le/la très honorable* in French). This was originally subject to being summoned to the British Privy Council. Several early prime ministers were not summoned to the British Privy Council, and hence were styled *The Honourable*: [Alexander Mackenzie](/wiki/Alexander_Mackenzie_(politician) "Alexander Mackenzie (politician)"), Sir [John Abbott](/wiki/John_Abbott "John Abbott") and Sir [Mackenzie Bowell](/wiki/Mackenzie_Bowell "Mackenzie Bowell").

Members of the [Executive Council of Quebec](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Quebec "Executive Council of Quebec") have not used the style *The Honourable* since 1968 but retain the ability to do so, and are often accorded the honorific in media and by the federal government.

#### The Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: The Caribbean")]

##### Caricom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Caricom")]

Members of the [Order of the Caribbean Community](/wiki/Order_of_the_Caribbean_Community "Order of the Caribbean Community") are entitled to be styled *The Honourable* for life.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

##### Barbados

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Barbados")]

In [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"), members of the Parliament carry two main titles: members of the [House of Assembly](/wiki/Barbados_House_of_Assembly "Barbados House of Assembly") are styled *The Honourable*, while members of the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Barbados "Senate of Barbados") are styled "Senator". Companions of Honour of the former [Order of Barbados](/wiki/Order_of_Barbados "Order of Barbados") from the pre-republic era of Barbados, as well Members of the current [Order of the Republic](/wiki/Order_of_Barbados "Order of Barbados"), are accorded the style *The Honourable*.

##### Jamaica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Jamaica")]

In [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), those awarded the [Order of Jamaica](/wiki/Order_of_Jamaica "Order of Jamaica") (considered Jamaica's equivalent to a British [knighthood](/wiki/Knighthood "Knighthood")) and those awarded the [Order of Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Merit_(Jamaica) "Order of Merit (Jamaica)") are styled *Honourable*.

#### Trinidad and Tobago

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Trinidad and Tobago")]

In [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago") The [Prime Minister](/wiki/List_of_prime_ministers_of_Trinidad_and_Tobago "List of prime ministers of Trinidad and Tobago"), government ministers, the leader of the opposition and ministers within government ministries (junior ministers) are styled as *The Honourable*, senators serving as ministers are styled as *Senator The Honourable*, ministers with doctorates are styled as *The Honourable Dr. or Dr. the Honourable (rare).*

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: United States")]

In the United States, the prefix *the Honorable* has been used to formally address various officials at the federal and state levels, but it is most commonly used for the President-elect, [governors](/wiki/Governor_(United_States) "Governor (United States)"), judges, and members of [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") when formally addressing them.[[21]](#cite_note-HickeyHowToUse-21) The style may be conferred pursuant to federal government service, according to federal rules, or by state government service, where the rules may be different. Modifiers such as *the Right Honorable* or *the Most Honorable* are not used. The 't' in 'the' is not capitalized in the middle of a sentence.[[22]](#cite_note-Hickey-22)

Under the rules of etiquette, the President, Vice President, members of both houses of Congress, [governors](/wiki/Governor_(United_States) "Governor (United States)") of states, members of [state legislatures](/wiki/State_legislature_(United_States) "State legislature (United States)"), and mayors are accorded the title.[[23]](#cite_note-Mewborn-23) Persons appointed to office [nominated by the President and confirmed by the Senate](/wiki/List_of_positions_filled_by_presidential_appointment_with_Senate_confirmation "List of positions filled by presidential appointment with Senate confirmation") are accorded the title; this rule includes members of the [Cabinet](/wiki/United_States_Cabinet "United States Cabinet") and sub-Cabinet (such as deputies and undersecretaries),[[23]](#cite_note-Mewborn-23)[[24]](#cite_note-google1-24) administrators, members, and commissioners of the various [independent agencies](/wiki/Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_government "Independent agencies of the United States government"), councils, commissions, and boards,[[24]](#cite_note-google1-24) [federal judges](/wiki/United_States_federal_judges "United States federal judges"), [ambassadors of the United States](/wiki/Ambassadors_of_the_United_States "Ambassadors of the United States"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) [U.S. Attorneys](/wiki/United_States_Attorney "United States Attorney"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) [U.S. Marshals](/wiki/United_States_Marshals_Service "United States Marshals Service"),[[27]](#cite_note-27) the [Architect of the Capitol](/wiki/Architect_of_the_Capitol "Architect of the Capitol"), the [Librarian of Congress](/wiki/Librarian_of_Congress "Librarian of Congress") and [Public Printer of the United States](/wiki/Public_Printer_of_the_United_States "Public Printer of the United States"),[[24]](#cite_note-google1-24) and presidentially appointed [inspectors general](/wiki/Inspector_General "Inspector General").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

High state officials other than governor, such as [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(United_States) "Lieutenant governor (United States)")[[29]](#cite_note-29) and [state attorneys general](/wiki/State_attorney_general "State attorney general")[[30]](#cite_note-30) are also accorded the style *Honorable*. [State court](/wiki/State_court_(United_States) "State court (United States)") judges and [justices of the peace](/wiki/Justices_of_the_peace "Justices of the peace"), like federal judges, also are accorded the style *Honorable*.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Practices vary on whether appointed state officials, such as the heads of state cabinet-level departments are given the title.[[23]](#cite_note-Mewborn-23)[[22]](#cite_note-Hickey-22) There is also no universal rule for whether county or city officials other than the mayor (such as [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council"), [board of aldermen](/wiki/Board_of_aldermen "Board of aldermen"), [board of education](/wiki/Board_of_education "Board of education"), [planning and zoning commission](/wiki/Planning_and_zoning_commission "Planning and zoning commission"), and code enforcement board members, or [city manager](/wiki/City_manager "City manager") or [police chief](/wiki/Police_chief "Police chief") or [fire chief](/wiki/Fire_chief "Fire chief")) are given the title; as these may be different state by state.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Members of the [White House staff](/wiki/Executive_Office_of_the_President_of_the_United_States "Executive Office of the President of the United States") at the rank of special assistant, deputy assistant, assistant to the president, and [Counselor to the President](/wiki/Counselor_to_the_President "Counselor to the President") are accorded the title. Officials nominated to high office but not yet confirmed (e.g., commissioner-designate) and interim or acting officials are generally not accorded the style *Honorable*, except for cabinet-level officials.[[21]](#cite_note-HickeyHowToUse-21)

Opinions vary on whether the term *the Honorable* is accorded for life.[[23]](#cite_note-Mewborn-23) According to the protocols of the [U.S. Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State"), all persons who have been in a position that entitled them to *The Honorable* continue to retain that honorific style for life.[[33]](#cite_note-33) However, the State Department is not an authority on state and local officials such as mayors, members of state legislatures, and high state officials. The prefix is not used for people who have died.[[22]](#cite_note-Hickey-22)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Some estimate that in the United States there are nearly 100,000 people who are accorded the "Honorable" title, many in the Washington, D.C. region.[[23]](#cite_note-Mewborn-23) Civilian officials, including service secretaries (e.g., [Secretary of the Army](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Army "United States Secretary of the Army")) of [the Pentagon](/wiki/The_Pentagon "The Pentagon") receive the title.[[24]](#cite_note-google1-24)

The style *The Honorable* is used on envelopes when referring to an individual in the third person. It is never properly used to refer to oneself.[[22]](#cite_note-Hickey-22)

A spouse of someone with the style of the *Honorable* receives no additional title.

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Oceania")]

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Australia")]

In Australia, the style is allowed to be used by past and present:[[36]](#cite_note-Australian_Style_Guide-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

* Governors-General and Governors
* Members of the [Federal Executive Council](/wiki/Federal_Executive_Council_(Australia) "Federal Executive Council (Australia)")
* Premiers and ministers in all states
* [Presiding officers](/wiki/Chairperson "Chairperson") of federal and state parliaments (except [ACT Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory_Legislative_Assembly "Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly") speakers)
* Members of all state legislative councils except in Victoria
* Chief ministers and ministers in the Northern Territory
* Leaders of the Opposition in Tasmania
* High Court Justices
* Federal Court Justices
* Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia, Division 1 Justices
* Justices of the supreme court in states and territories.

The abbreviation in Australia is '**The Hon'** (without a full stop).[[36]](#cite_note-Australian_Style_Guide-36)

##### Governors-General

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Governors-General")]

In May 2013, the title was given approval by Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") to be granted to past, present, and future [Governors-General of Australia](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia"),[[38]](#cite_note-hongg-38) to be used in the form *His* or *Her Excellency the Honourable* while holding office, and *The Honourable* in retirement.

##### Governors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Governors")]

By December 2014, the practice of appointing the vice-regal office holder, as well as former living, the style *The Honourable* for life had also been adopted for the state governors of [New South Wales](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales"), [Queensland](/wiki/Governor_of_Queensland "Governor of Queensland"), [South Australia](/wiki/Governor_of_South_Australia "Governor of South Australia"), [Western Australia](/wiki/Governor_of_Western_Australia "Governor of Western Australia"), [Victoria](/wiki/Governor_of_Victoria "Governor of Victoria") and [Tasmania](/wiki/Governor_of_Tasmania "Governor of Tasmania") (where it did not apply to past governors), as well as the [Administrator of the Northern Territory](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Northern_Territory "Administrator of the Northern Territory").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

##### Government ministers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Government ministers")]

In Australia, all ministers in Commonwealth and state governments and the government of the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") are entitled to be styled *the Honourable*. The [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") does not have an [executive council](/wiki/Executive_Council_(Commonwealth_countries) "Executive Council (Commonwealth countries)") and so its ministers are not entitled to the style. In Victoria, the style is granted for life, so it is customary for former ministers to retain the style after leaving office.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) With respect to New South Wales, Queensland,[[41]](#cite_note-41) South Australia and Tasmania the [King-in-Council](/wiki/King-in-Council "King-in-Council") may grant former ministers the style for life. The same principle applies in the Northern Territory via the [chief minister](/wiki/Chief_minister "Chief minister"), to the administrator, to the governor-general, then to the King. A minimum five years' service as a member of the executive council and/or as a presiding officer is a prerequisite. In Western Australia, conditional on royal assent, the style may become permanent after three years' service in the ministry.[[42]](#cite_note-42) All such awards are published in the *Commonwealth Government Gazette*. The presiding officers of the parliaments of the Commonwealth, the states and the Northern Territory are also styled *the Honourable*, but normally only during their tenure of office. Special permission is sometimes given for a former presiding officer to retain the style after leaving office, as is the case in the Northern Territory.

##### Members of Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Members of Parliament")]

The title *Honourable* is not acquired through membership of either the House of Representatives or the Senate (see [Parliament of Australia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia")). A member or senator may have the style if they have acquired it separately, e.g. by being a current or former minister.[[43]](#cite_note-43) During proceedings within the chambers, forms such as "the honourable member for ...", "the honourable Leader of the Opposition", or "my honourable colleague" are used. This is a parliamentary courtesy and does not imply any right to the style.

Traditionally, members of the [legislative councils](/wiki/Legislative_council "Legislative council") of the states have been styled *the Honourable* for the duration of their terms. That practice is still followed in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "New South Wales Legislative Council"), [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australian_Legislative_Council "Western Australian Legislative Council"), [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australian_Legislative_Council "South Australian Legislative Council") and [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmanian_Legislative_Council "Tasmanian Legislative Council"). In [Victoria](/wiki/Victorian_Legislative_Council "Victorian Legislative Council"), the practice was abolished in 2003. In New South Wales, [Greens NSW](/wiki/Greens_New_South_Wales "Greens New South Wales") members of the [Legislative Council](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "New South Wales Legislative Council"), who are eligible for *the Honourable* style, have refrained from using it, deeming it to be "outdated" and a "colonial trapping".[[44]](#cite_note-44)

##### Judges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Judges")]

Judges of all [superior courts](/wiki/Superior_court "Superior court") are also referred to formally by the style *the Honourable*, both during and after holding the office.

#### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

The style *The Honourable* was first granted in 1854 for use by members of the [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_New_Zealand "Executive Council of New Zealand"), the [Speaker of the Legislative Council](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_New_Zealand_Legislative_Council "Speaker of the New Zealand Legislative Council"), the Members of the [Legislative Council](/wiki/New_Zealand_Legislative_Council "New Zealand Legislative Council"), and the [Speaker of the House of Representatives](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_New_Zealand_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the New Zealand House of Representatives").[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In July 2006, the [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand") (and former living Governors-General) were granted the use of the title *The Honourable* for life, unless they already held the title *[The Right Honourable](/wiki/Right_Honourable_(New_Zealand) "Right Honourable (New Zealand)")* (via membership of the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)")).[[46]](#cite_note-nz2006-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) This title was also granted to [Prime Ministers](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand "Prime Minister of New Zealand"), [Chief Justices](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_New_Zealand "Chief Justice of New Zealand"), and [Speakers](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_New_Zealand_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the New Zealand House of Representatives") of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_Zealand "Parliament of New Zealand"), along with judges of the [Supreme](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_New_Zealand "Supreme Court of New Zealand"), [Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_New_Zealand "Court of Appeal of New Zealand"), and [High](/wiki/High_Court_of_New_Zealand "High Court of New Zealand") Courts of New Zealand - albeit only during their tenures in these offices, unless they already held the title *Right Honourable*.[[46]](#cite_note-nz2006-46) These officeholders would be eligible for a recommendation (from the Prime Minister) to retain these titles for life following their relinquishment of/retirement from those offices. Furthermore, authority for considering these recommendations was now delegated to the Governor-General, rather than [the monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_New_Zealand "Monarchy of New Zealand").[[46]](#cite_note-nz2006-46)

The rules were amended again in 2010, granting the title of *[The Right Honourable](/wiki/Right_Honourable_(New_Zealand) "Right Honourable (New Zealand)")* for life to sitting and future Governors-General, Prime Ministers, Speakers of the House of Representatives, and Chief Justices.[[48]](#cite_note-nz2010-48) It also permitted these officeholders to use the letters 'PC' after their name to denote membership of the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)"), if they are privy counsellors.[[48]](#cite_note-nz2010-48) However, it granted the Prime Minister the power to strip the title of *Right Honourable* from these titleholders, via issuing such [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional_law) "Advice (constitutional law)") to the monarch.[[48]](#cite_note-nz2010-48)

New Zealand office holders who are *The Honourable* ex-officio are usually granted the style for life by the [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand") as a courtesy when they vacate the office;[[49]](#cite_note-49) all honours and awards are published in *[The New Zealand Gazette](/wiki/The_New_Zealand_Gazette "The New Zealand Gazette")*.

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Asia")]

#### East Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: East Asia")]

##### *South Korea*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: South Korea")]

In South Korea, the prefix *The Honourable* is used for the following people:

* Ministerial Members of [Cabinet of Korea](/wiki/State_Council_of_South_Korea "State Council of South Korea")
* Leaders and Floor Leaders of Parties and Members of Shadow Cabinet.
* Governors and Ministerial members of Provinces
* Mayors of Metropolitan Cities
* Judges at [Supreme Court of Korea](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Korea "Supreme Court of Korea") and Chief Justices of Provincial Level Courts.

##### *Hong Kong*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

In Hong Kong, the prefix *The Honourable* is used for the following people:

* The [Chief Executive of Hong Kong](/wiki/Chief_Executive_of_Hong_Kong "Chief Executive of Hong Kong")
* Members, including the President, of the [Legislative Council](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong")
* Members of the [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Executive Council of Hong Kong"), including official members such as the [Chief Secretary](/wiki/Chief_Secretary_for_Administration "Chief Secretary for Administration"), the [Financial Secretary](/wiki/Financial_Secretary_(Hong_Kong) "Financial Secretary (Hong Kong)"), the [Secretary for Justice](/wiki/Secretary_for_Justice_(Hong_Kong) "Secretary for Justice (Hong Kong)"), and other [secretaries of bureaux](/wiki/Principal_officials_of_Hong_Kong "Principal officials of Hong Kong")
* Judges of the [Court of Final Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Final_Appeal_(Hong_Kong) "Court of Final Appeal (Hong Kong)")
* Justices of Appeal of the [Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_(Hong_Kong) "Court of Appeal (Hong Kong)")
* Judges of the [Court of First Instance](/wiki/Court_of_First_Instance_(Hong_Kong) "Court of First Instance (Hong Kong)")
* Individuals who have been awarded the [Grand Bauhinia Medal](/wiki/Hong_Kong_honours_system "Hong Kong honours system"), the highest medal in Hong Kong's honours system.
* Deceased heroes (29 to date) who served in any of the [disciplined services](/wiki/Hong_Kong_disciplined_services "Hong Kong disciplined services") under the [Security Bureau](/wiki/Security_Bureau_(Hong_Kong) "Security Bureau (Hong Kong)")

##### *Macau*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Macau")]

In [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), the prefix *The Honourable* is used occasionally for the following people:

* [Chief Executive of Macau](/wiki/Chief_Executive_of_Macau "Chief Executive of Macau")
* Members, including the President, of the [Legislative Assembly of Macau](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Macau "Legislative Assembly of Macau")
* Members of the [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Macau "Executive Council of Macau")
* The [Secretariat for Administration and Justice (Macau)](/wiki/Secretariat_for_Administration_and_Justice_(Macau) "Secretariat for Administration and Justice (Macau)"), the [Secretariat for Economy and Finance (Macau)](/wiki/Secretariat_for_Economy_and_Finance_(Macau) "Secretariat for Economy and Finance (Macau)"), and other Principal officials
* Judges of the [Court of Final Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Final_Appeal_(Macau) "Court of Final Appeal (Macau)")
* Individuals who have been awarded the [Grand Medal of Lotus Flower](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_Macau "Orders, decorations, and medals of Macau"), the highest medal in Macau's honours system.

#### South Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: South Asia")]

##### *Bangladesh*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Bangladesh")]

In the [People's Republic of Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_Bangladesh "Chief Justice of Bangladesh"), [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Bangladesh "Supreme Court of Bangladesh") Justices,[[50]](#cite_note-50) [House Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Jatiya_Sangsad "Speaker of the Jatiya Sangsad"), [Ministers](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Bangladesh "Cabinet of Bangladesh"), [Members of Parliaments](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Bangladesh) "Member of Parliament (Bangladesh)"), [Interim Advisers](/wiki/Adviser_(Interim_government_of_Bangladesh) "Adviser (Interim government of Bangladesh)"), [PM Advisers](/wiki/Adviser_to_the_Prime_Minister_of_Bangladesh "Adviser to the Prime Minister of Bangladesh"), VVIPs and the [Mayor](/wiki/List_of_city_corporations_in_Bangladesh#List_of_city_mayors "List of city corporations in Bangladesh")[[51]](#cite_note-51) are entitled to the style *Honourable*. On the other hand, the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Bangladesh "Prime Minister of Bangladesh"), [Chief Adviser of Bangladesh](/wiki/Chief_Adviser_of_Bangladesh "Chief Adviser of Bangladesh")[[52]](#cite_note-52) and the [President](/wiki/President_of_Bangladesh "President of Bangladesh") are styled *Honourable* or *[Excellency](/wiki/Excellency "Excellency")*.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

##### *India*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: India")]

In India, judges of the [high courts](/wiki/High_courts_of_India "High courts of India") and [Supreme Court of India](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_India "Supreme Court of India") are addressed as *Honourable* *(*Hon'ble*)*;[[55]](#cite_note-55) often stylized and abbreviated as "HMJ", i.e., Honourable Mr./Ms. Justice, followed by their name.

The elected legislators ([members of legislative assembly](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly_(India) "Member of the Legislative Assembly (India)"), [members of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(India) "Member of Parliament (India)")) and heads of government (The [President](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India"), The [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_India "Prime Minister of India"), [union ministers](/wiki/Union_Council_of_Ministers "Union Council of Ministers"), [chief ministers](/wiki/Chief_minister_(India) "Chief minister (India)"), and [governors](/wiki/Governor_(India) "Governor (India)")) are also formally called *Honourable* followed by their name. The [Vice President of India](/wiki/Vice_President_of_India "Vice President of India") is addressed as the hon'ble as well.

##### *Pakistan*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

In Pakistan, the judicial officers are addressed as honourable while presiding over in the courts of law. It is a norm to address judges of superior judiciary as honourable judges. Diplomats are addressed as Your Excellency. The head of state and Prime Minister is addressed her/his excellency.

##### *Sri Lanka*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Sri Lanka")]

In [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), the honorific *The Honourable* is used to refer to the President, Prime Minister, Ministers, and Members of Parliament. [Attorney-General](/wiki/Attorney-General_of_Sri_Lanka "Attorney-General of Sri Lanka") and [Solicitor-General](/wiki/Solicitor_General_of_Sri_Lanka "Solicitor General of Sri Lanka") as well as Judges of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Sri_Lanka "Supreme Court of Sri Lanka"), the [Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_Sri_Lanka "Court of Appeal of Sri Lanka") and the [High Courts](/wiki/High_Court_of_Sri_Lanka "High Court of Sri Lanka").[[56]](#cite_note-56)

#### Southeast Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Southeast Asia")]

##### *Malaysia*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Malaysia")]

Main article: [Malay titles § Honorary styles](/wiki/Malay_titles#Honorary_styles "Malay titles")

In [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), The Honourable (The Hon) or Yang Berhormat (YB) – the style of members of parliament and state legislative assemblymen. The prefix Yang Berhormat is also used for recipients of the First and Second Classes of the [Johor's Orders of Chivalry](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_Malaysian_states_and_federal_territories#Johor "Orders, decorations, and medals of the Malaysian states and federal territories") who is titled Dato' for men and Datin Paduka for women, regardless of whether a member of parliament or not.

##### *Myanmar*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Myanmar")]

In [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), the Chief Justice and Justices of the [Supreme Court of Myanmar](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Myanmar "Supreme Court of Myanmar") are referred as 'The Honourable'.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

##### *Philippines*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Philippines")]

In the Philippines, the style is usually used to give distinction to any elected official (whether in office or retired) ranging from the smallest political unit, the *[barangay](/wiki/Barangay "Barangay")*, to the [Congress of the Philippines](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Philippines "Congress of the Philippines"), which consists of the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Philippines "Senate of the Philippines") and [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_of_the_Philippines "House of Representatives of the Philippines").[[58]](#cite_note-UP_hnbk-58) Appointed officials such as members of the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_Philippines "Cabinet of the Philippines") (secretaries, acting secretaries, ad interim secretaries, undersecretaries, and assistant secretaries), the [Solicitor General](/wiki/Office_of_the_Solicitor_General_of_the_Philippines "Office of the Solicitor General of the Philippines"), and heads of government agencies at the national and local levels are also accorded this style.[[59]](#cite_note-UP_hnbk_2-59) For example, a *[kagawad](/wiki/Barangay_Kagawad "Barangay Kagawad")* ([barangay](/wiki/Barangay "Barangay") or village council member) named [Juan de la Cruz](/wiki/Juan_de_la_Cruz "Juan de la Cruz") will be referred to as *The Honorable Juan de la Cruz*. In written form, the style may be shortened to "Hon." (e.g. *Hon. Juan de la Cruz*).

The [vice-president](/wiki/Vice-President_of_the_Philippines "Vice-President of the Philippines"), [chief justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_Philippines "Chief Justice of the Philippines"), [ombudsman](/wiki/Ombudsman_of_the_Philippines "Ombudsman of the Philippines"), justices of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_Philippines "Supreme Court of the Philippines"), [Sandiganbayan](/wiki/Sandiganbayan "Sandiganbayan"), and [Court of Appeals](/wiki/Court_of_Appeals_of_the_Philippines "Court of Appeals of the Philippines"), and trial court judges are also addressed in this honorific style.[[58]](#cite_note-UP_hnbk-58) Meanwhile, the [president](/wiki/President_of_the_Philippines "President of the Philippines") of the Philippines, members of the diplomatic corps, Filipino diplomats, and Catholic archbishops is always given the style [His/Her Excellency](/wiki/Excellency "Excellency").[[58]](#cite_note-UP_hnbk-58) Cardinal-Archbishops of the Catholic Church are addressed as [His Eminence](/wiki/Eminence_(style) "Eminence (style)").

##### *Singapore*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Singapore")]

The [chief justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_Singapore "Chief Justice of Singapore"), judges of appeal, and justices of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Singapore "Supreme Court of Singapore"),[[60]](#cite_note-60) and the Presiding Judge and District Judges of the [State Courts](/wiki/State_Courts_of_Singapore "State Courts of Singapore")[[61]](#cite_note-61) are conventionally addressed in formal settings using the honorific *The Honourable*.

All former [Prime Ministers](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Singapore "Prime Minister of Singapore") and current [Members of the Singapore Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Singapore "Parliament of Singapore") is formally addressed in international settings using the honorific *The Honourable*.

The use of the honorific *The Honourable* to refer to the Prime Minister, Ministers, and Members of Parliament is not required by the Standing Orders of Parliament,[[62]](#cite_note-62) but during a 1988 parliamentary debate the [Leader of the House](/wiki/Parliament_of_Singapore#Leader_of_the_House "Parliament of Singapore"), [Wong Kan Seng](/wiki/Wong_Kan_Seng "Wong Kan Seng"), said it would be polite for MPs to refer to their colleagues using the terms "Mr.", "Honourable Mr." or "Honourable Minister" depending on their choice.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

The honorific is usually also used to address the [Attorney-General](/wiki/Attorney-General_of_Singapore "Attorney-General of Singapore") and Solicitors-General, and the heads of states and leaders of foreign countries on short-term visits to Singapore.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

## Non-governmental use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Honourable&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Non-governmental use")]

Private, non-profit, and non-governmental (NGO) organisations, and religious movements sometimes style a leader or founder as *The Honourable*, e.g. *The Honourable [Elijah Muhammad](/wiki/Elijah_Muhammad "Elijah Muhammad")*.

The [Heralds' College](/wiki/College_of_Arms "College of Arms") officially reported on the petition on October 31, 1835, stating (...) that "the style of 'The Honourable' is given to the Judges and to the [Barons of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chief_Baron_of_the_Exchequer "Chief Baron of the Exchequer"), with others; because, by the Decree of the tenth of [King James the First](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), for settling the place and precedency of the [Baronets](/wiki/Baronet "Baronet"), the Judges, and Barons of the Exchequer, were declared to have place and precedence before the younger sons of [Viscounts](/wiki/Viscount "Viscount") and [Barons](/wiki/Baron "Baron")."  
   (...) The analogy held only in so far as both styles were applicable to those who belonged to the less exalted ranks of the titled classes, for the title "honourable" was not definitely confined to certain classes until later.  
    The terms *honorabilis* and *honorabilitas* were in use in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") as a form of politeness rather than as a specific title. More than two centuries later [John Selden](/wiki/John_Selden "John Selden"), in his Titles of Honor (1614), does not include "honourable" among the courtesy titles given to the children of peers.  
   The style was, in fact, used extremely loosely until well on into the 18th century.  
   (...) British baronets, for instance, claimed that they had been styled "the honourable" until the end of the 18th century, and in 1835 they petitioned for the style as a prefix to their names. The Heralds' College officially reported on the petition on October 31, 1835, stating that the presented evidence did not prove the right of baronets to the style and that its use "has been no more warranted by authority than when the same style has been applied to [Field Officers](/wiki/Field_Officer "Field Officer") in the Army and others.".  
   (...) It is not, indeed, until 1874 that there is any clear evidence of an authoritative limitation of the title. In this year the wives of [Lords of Appeal](/wiki/Lords_of_Appeal_in_Ordinary "Lords of Appeal in Ordinary") were granted style and precedence as baronesses, but it was provided that their children were not "to assume or use the prefix of Honourable, or to be entitled to the style, rank or precedence of the children of a Baron." In 1898, however, this was revoked, and it was ordained "that such children shall have and enjoy on all occasions the style and title enjoyed by the children of hereditary Barons together with the rank and precedence." By these acts of the Crown, the prefix "honourable" would seem to have been restricted as a definite title of honour, yet in legal documents the sons of peers are still styled merely "[esquire](/wiki/Esquire "Esquire")". This latter fact points to the time when the prefix "honourable" was a mark of deference paid by others rather than a style assumed by right."
